sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=33, col=15), MOUSE(row=33, col=21).
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 193, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'ACTION7': "Invalid - gives 'Unknown action' error", 'LEFT': 'Moves player left ~6 columns', 'MOUSE': 'Clicks position. Green blocks: collects but game over after 8-9 total clicks (persists across resets). Dots/purple blocks/platforms: adds 1 purple pixel to bottom bar. Bar clicks: adds 1 purple pixel.', 'RIGHT': 'Moves player right ~6 columns'}
- Object taxonomy: {'background': 'black with gray dots', 'dots': 'yellow/white dots at row 11 below purple blocks', 'green_blocks': 'light green 21-pixel blocks in 2x7 grid', 'player': 'blue+yellow small object', 'platforms': 'large sky blue regions', 'purple_blocks_top': '3 purple blocks at rows 7-10'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'Starts empty, accumulates 1 purple pixel per click. Resets on game over. Currently 15 pixels.'}
- Win pattern: {'level1': 'Collect green blocks with MOUSE, move LEFT toward pink cross to complete level', 'level2': 'UNKNOWN - all attempts to collect 14 green blocks fail with game over after 8-9 clicks'}
- Level log: {'level1': 'Completed - collect greens with MOUSE, approach pink cross, LEFT to complete', 'level2': 'Multiple game overs. Green counter persists. Bar accumulates from clicks. Testing bar=14 hypothesis.'}
- Cross-level notes: Bar accumulates purple from ANY click. Green click counter persists across game overs. Level 1: collect greens, LEFT to pink cross. Level 2: unknown solution - green counter persists.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 142 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 3.0x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 13 actions returned the board to a state already seen 13 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 13 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 6 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Game over triggered when bar reached 12 (started at 10, clicked 2 greens → bar 11, 12 → game over). So the threshold is 12.
Key findings:
- Bar threshold for game over is 12
- Each green click adds 1 to bar
- To collect 14 greens, bar would go from 0 to 14, which exceeds 12
- So I can collect at most 12 greens in one session (bar 0→12)
New hypothesis: Maybe I need to collect greens in batches:
- Session 1: collect 12 greens (bar 0→12, game over)
- Session 2: collect remaining 2 greens (bar 0→2)
- Then do something else (like LEFT like in level 1)?
Or maybe the solution involves the player character. In level 1, after collecting greens, I moved LEFT to a pink cross. What if in level 2, I need to:
1. Collect some greens
2. Move the player to a specific position
3. Click something else
Wait, let me reconsider. What if the bar threshold IS the goal? Like, I need to fill the bar to exactly some value without going over?
Or what if I need to collect exactly 7 greens (half of 14)? The first time I collected 7 greens successfully (bar went 3→10). Maybe 7 is the target?
Let me think about what happens after collecting 7 greens. In level 1, after collecting greens, I moved LEFT to a pink cross. What if in level 2, after collecting 7 greens, I need to do something with the player?
Actually, let me try a completely fresh approach. After game over, the board resets. Let me:
1. Collect exactly 7 greens (bar 0→7)
2. Move the player LEFT or RIGHT
3. See if something special happens
Or maybe I should try: collect 7 greens, then click the yellow/white dots to add to the bar to reach some target value.
Wait, I just realized something. In level 1, the solution was: collect greens, then LEFT to pink cross. The pink cross was a TARGET to reach. In level 2, maybe the purple blocks are targets? Or the dots?
Let me try: collect 7 greens (one row), then move player to a specific position and see if there's a target.
Actually, let me first check the current board state after game over.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game over at bar=12. Threshold confirmed: 12.
# Can collect max 12 greens per session.
# Need new approach. Let me check current state after game over.

seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
bar = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p' and n['boundary'][0][0] == 63]
player = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']

print(f"Greens: {len(greens)}, Bar: {[n['pixels'] for n in bar]}, Player: {len(player)}")

# Check if board fully reset
for n in seg['nodes'][:5]:
    print(f"  {n['color']}: {n['pixels']} pixels")

# New plan: collect 7 greens (bar 0→7), then try LEFT/RIGHT like level 1
# First, get green positions
green_centers = []
for g in greens:
    rows = [p[0] for p in g['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in g['boundary']]
    r = (min(rows) + max(rows)) // 2
    c = (min(cols) + max(cols)) // 2
    green_centers.append((r, c))
green_centers.sort()

# Collect top 7 greens (first row)
for i, (r, c) in enumerate(green_centers[:7]):
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c}])
    if last_action_result.get('game_over') or last_action_result.get('done'):
        print(f"Game over after click {i+1}")
        break
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    remaining = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
    bar = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p' and n['boundary'][0][0] == 63]
    print(f"Click {i+1}: {len(remaining)} greens, bar={[n['pixels'] for n in bar]}")

# Now try LEFT like level 1
if not last_action_result.get('game_over'):
    print("Trying LEFT...")
    action(['LEFT'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
    bar = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p' and n['boundary'][0][0] == 63]
    print(f"After LEFT: {len(greens)} greens, bar={[n['pixels'] for n in bar]}")
    print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}, Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"ACTION7": "Invalid", "LEFT": "Moves player left ~6 columns", "MOUSE": "Green: collects + adds 1 to bar. Other: adds 1 to bar. Game over at bar=12.", "RIGHT": "Moves player right ~6 columns"}, "cross_level_notes": "Bar threshold=12 triggers game over. Level 1: collect greens then LEFT to pink cross.", "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "Resets on game over. Threshold=12."}, "level_log": {"level2": "Collected 7 greens (bar 3→10), then game over at bar=12. Testing collect-then-LEFT."}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Collect greens, LEFT to pink cross"}}, "level_tier": {"current_plan": "Collect 7 greens (bar 0→7), then LEFT like level 1", "failed_probes": ["Collect all 14 - bar threshold blocks", "Purple block clicks"], "goal_model": "Collect 7 greens then LEFT to target", "recent_findings": ["Bar threshold=12 triggers game over", "Collected 7 greens successfully with bar 3→10"], "world_model": "Board reset after game over, 14 greens, bar=0"}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
